coredata.dc:title: SELEX-derived DNA aptamer utilized for sensitive electrochemical biosensing of Toxoplasma gondii surface antigen 1
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kanagavalli, Pandiyaraj
Alkhaldi, Shahad
Zourob, Mohammed
Eissa, Shimaa
coredata.dc:description: Toxoplasma gondii (T. gondii) is a global parasitic pathogen with significant health implications. Effective diagnosis is crucial, especially for vulnerable populations like immunocompromised individuals and pregnant women. Here, we developed a novel electrochemical aptasensor for detecting Surface Antigen 1 (SAG1), a key biomarker for acute T. gondii infections. New high-affinity aptamers (SOK3, SOK14, and SOK18) specific to SAG1 were identified using SELEX method. The aptamers were then immobilized onto screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPE) modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Physicochemical characterization confirmed successful aptasensor fabrication. SOK14 was identified as optimal for SAG1 detection due to its lower dissociation constant and distinct current response upon protein binding. Square wave voltammetry (SWV) showed a linear response from 0.01 to 100 nM SAG1 (n = 3), correlating with [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− oxidation peak current changes and achieving a low detection limit of 11.5 pM with relative standard deviations ranging from 3.3 to 4.9 %. Selectivity studies against other nonspecific biomarkers for other pathogens validated the aptasensor's specificity. Application of the aptasensor in spiked serum samples indicated its efficiency in complex biological samples. This platform enables rapid, on-site detection of T. gondii with high sensitivity, selectivity, and reliability in serum samples, supporting early diagnosis and timely treatment to prevent complications.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Khalifa University 2024: Hybrid and Gold Journals (Prepaid Pot)
coredata.dcterms:subject: SELEX method
Toxoplasma gondii
Surface antigen 1
Graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical aptasensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of dissociation constant of SAG1 aptamers
Cloning and sequencing of the aptamers
Assessment of aptamer binding affinity via fluorescence assays
Physicochemical characterization of the fabricated aptasensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: S. Eissa acknowledges Khalifa University of Science and Technology, United Arab Emirates, for the financial support provided through start-up grant number 8474000456 . M. Zourob acknowledges the support from Alfaisal University , Riyadh, KSA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Evaluation of the DNA recovery by measuring fluorescence intensities of the elusions after each SELEX selection cycle. Secondary structures of the aptamers obtained by Mfold for (b) SOK3, (c) SOK14, and (d) SOK18. (e) Saturation curve illustrates the calculation of the dissociation constant for the SOK14 aptamer (a plot of aptamer concentration versus fluorescence intensity). Error bars indicate the standard deviation from three measurements.
(a) Raman spectra of bare SPE and GQDs/SPE. SEM images of (b) bare SPE and (c) GQDs modified SPE.
(a) XPS survey spectra of SPE, GQD/SPE and aptamer/GQD/SPE. C 1s deconvoluted spectra of (b) SPE, (c) GQDs/SPE and (d) Aptamer/GQDs/SPE. (e) The N 1s spectra of Apt/GQDs/SPE.

CVs of electrochemical aptasensor platform constructed on GQDs/SPE with different aptamers: SOK3 (a), SOK18 (b) and SOK14 (c). The SWV signals of SOK 14 aptasensors (d) (GQDs/SPE -blue, aptamer/GQDs/SPE -red, and SAG1/Apt/GQDs/SPE -black).

(a) SWVs of GQDs/SPE incubated with different concentrations of SAG 1 aptamer for 60 min. (b) represents the percentage of the peak current change vs the SAG1 aptamer concentration. (c) SWVs of GQDs/SPE after incubation with 20 μM SAG 1 aptamer at different time intervals. (d) Histogram of the percentage of peak current changes vs immobilization time of SAG 1 aptamer on GQDs/SPE. The measurements were performed in triplicated and the error bars represent the standard deviations of the measurements.

(a) SWVs of bare GQDs/SPE, aptamer (SOK14)-functionalized GQDs/SPE, and after SAG1 protein incubation (Aptamer concentration: 20 μM; SAG1 concentrations: 0.01–100 nM). (b) Derivative plot of the aptasensor responses related to the logarithm of SAG1 concentration vs. the oxidation peak current changes (n = 3). (e) Histogram represents the aptasensor response to 1 nM of SAG1 and 10 nM of interferents such as IFV A, IFV B, and RSV B (n = 3). The error bars represent the standard deviations of the measurements.

(a) Schematic representation of the selection of the aptamers using SELEX cycles. (b) Illustration of the construction of the electrochemical aptasensor and its working principle for SAG1 protein detection on the GQDs/SPE.
High-affinity binding aptamer sequences and their respective dissociation constants (Kd) to SAG1 protein.
Comparison of various methods for detecting SAG1 antigen and IgG antibodies for the diagnosis of Toxoplasma gondii.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DELAA - Direct enzyme-linked aptamer assay; ELISA – Enzyme linked immunosorbent assay; AuNPs-MUA - AuNPs functionalized with mercaptoundecanoic acid; PCR – Polymerase chain reaction; Q-DAS - quantum dots-labeled dual aptasensor; IU - International Units; CNFs/AuNPs - carbon nanofibers and gold nanoparticles; PVA/PVP/RGO/SPCE - poly-vinyl alcohol/poly-vinylpyrrolidone/reduced graphene oxide/screen-printed carbon electrode.

head.ce:title: SELEX-derived DNA aptamer utilized for sensitive electrochemical biosensing of Toxoplasma gondii surface antigen 1

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Khalifa University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Pandiyaraj Kanagavalli and Shahad Alkhaldi equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Toxoplasma gondii (T. gondii) is a global parasitic pathogen with significant health implications. Effective diagnosis is crucial, especially for vulnerable populations like immunocompromised individuals and pregnant women. Here, we developed a novel electrochemical aptasensor for detecting Surface Antigen 1 (SAG1), a key biomarker for acute T. gondii infections. New high-affinity aptamers (SOK3, SOK14, and SOK18) specific to SAG1 were identified using SELEX method. The aptamers were then immobilized onto screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPE) modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Physicochemical characterization confirmed successful aptasensor fabrication. SOK14 was identified as optimal for SAG1 detection due to its lower dissociation constant and distinct current response upon protein binding. Square wave voltammetry (SWV) showed a linear response from 0.01 to 100 nM SAG1 (n = 3), correlating with [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− oxidation peak current changes and achieving a low detection limit of 11.5 pM with relative standard deviations ranging from 3.3 to 4.9 %. Selectivity studies against other nonspecific biomarkers for other pathogens validated the aptasensor's specificity. Application of the aptasensor in spiked serum samples indicated its efficiency in complex biological samples. This platform enables rapid, on-site detection of T. gondii with high sensitivity, selectivity, and reliability in serum samples, supporting early diagnosis and timely treatment to prevent complications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxoplasma gondii (T. gondii), a widespread zoonotic parasite, poses a significant global health risk [1–3]. While often asymptomatic in individuals with robust immune systems, T. gondii infections can lead to severe complications in immunocompromised individuals, such as those with AIDS or undergoing chemotherapy, resulting in conditions like toxoplasmic encephalitis, retinochoroiditis, and even mortality [4–7]. Pregnant women are especially vulnerable, as T. gondii infection can cause spontaneous abortion, fetal death, and congenital abnormalities [8]. Timely diagnosis is crucial, particularly for at-risk populations, to mitigate morbidity and mortality.

Various diagnostic methods are used to detect T. gondii infection in humans, employing direct and indirect approaches [3,9]. Direct methods involve isolating the parasite through animal inoculation, tissue cultures, or immunoperoxidase staining and amplifying specific nucleic acid sequences [10–12]. Indirect methods primarily rely on serological tests to detect anti-toxoplasma antibodies (IgM, IgA, IgE, IgG, and IgG avidity) in serum samples [13–16]. These techniques are used to detect parasite antigens in body fluids, on skin, in environmental samples, and in amniotic fluid samples [3,17–22].

Traditional serological tests often employ whole tachyzoites or total extracts as antigens, which may not effectively distinguish between cerebral toxoplasmosis and asymptomatic cases. Consequently, clinical and imaging findings often guide treatment decisions due to limitations in antigen/antibody production, cost, and stability. Therefore, there is an urgent need for alternative sensitive, specific, and user-friendly diagnostic methods to effectively diagnose T. gondii infections and differentiate between chronic and acute cases.

Aptamers, derived from a synthetic oligonucleotide library via Systematic Evolution of Ligands by Exponential enrichment (SELEX), act as “chemical antibodies” offering a non-protein-based alternative to traditional antibodies [23]. These molecules are cost-effective, ethical, and highly specific and are easy to chemically modify, making them suitable for various assays [24]. Aptamers are particularly valuable for real-time on-site detection and have been proven useful in diagnosing and treating diseases [25,26]. We have previously reported the selection of several aptamers and their integration into biosensors for the diagnosis of diabetes [27], hormonal imbalances [28,29], and genetic disorders [30,31], demonstrating excellent applicability in real clinical samples. Moreover, aptamers have been integrated with molecular imprinted polymer and were utilized for multiple sensing applications, showing excellent performance [32–34]. For the detection of toxoplasma antibody, Luo et al. have selected two aptamers (TGA6 and TGA7) and utilized them as capture and detection probes in a fluorescence-based quantum dots-labeled dual aptasensor. This method showed good sensitivity, specificity, and affinity for anti-Toxoplasma IgG antibodies [35]. However, antibodies, especially IgG, are produced later in the course of infection, meaning this method may not be effective for early-stage detection. Despite their importance, the detection of toxoplasma antigens is limited. Vargas-Montes et al. have developed SELEX aptamers; AP001 and AP002 for an enzyme-linked aptamer assay (ELAA), showing high affinity and specificity for rhoptry protein 18 (ROP18), a key virulence factor of T. gondii. [36]. Detecting surface antigen 1 (SAG1) is theoretically straightforward due to its location and abundance on the surface of tachyzoites [37]. Cui et al. employed SELEX-derived aptamer to develop a direct ELAA specifically targeting SAG1 protein [38]. Their aptamer consisted of 53 nucleotides with a dissociation constant (Kd) of 41.57 nM. However, this method required the use of antibodies for detection and labeling reagents, which increased the overall cost. Further studies are needed to identify additional aptamers targeting the SAG1 protein that are suitable for use in label-free electrochemical biosensors. These label-free techniques offer advantages like cost-effectiveness, use of unlabeled DNA, high sensitivity, and potential for miniaturization in biosensing [39]. While electrochemical assays for detecting anti-toxoplasma antibodies (Ig G and Ig M) are established [15,35,40], no published studies exist on direct electrochemical biosensing methods for the SAG1 protein. Graphene and its derivatives, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are widely used in biosensing platforms due to their unique properties [41–43]. Their exceptional electrical conductivity, large surface area-to-volume ratio, and excellent biocompatibility make them ideal candidates for sensitive and selective detection of biomolecules. Additionally, GQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence, which can be exploited for fluorescence-based assays, enhancing the sensitivity and reliability of biosensing devices [44]. The graphene-like structure of GQDs imparts many of graphene's properties [45]. Their electrochemical behavior has been utilized to modify electrode surfaces [46] and serve as carrier tags for signal amplification in electrochemical immunosensors [47]. For instance, Tuteja et al. reported the detection of cardiac biomarkers using a GQD substrate [48], while Mollarasouli et al. reported the label-free electrochemical immunosensing of tyrosine kinase receptors using GQD-modified screen-printed electrodes (SPE) [49]. Previous research has widely explored graphene materials-aptamer combinations to detect biomarkers for infectious diseases [50–53]. The release of aptamers from graphene material surfaces upon target binding has been identified as a mechanism underlying various biosensing applications [52]. In this study, we present the selection and identification of a novel SAG1 aptamer with enhanced affinity and its application in developing an aptasensor. The aptamers were immobilized on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-modified screen-printed electrodes and monitored using square wave voltammetry (SWV) in a ferri/ferrocyanide redox system. Upon interaction with the target protein, the aptamer is released from the electrode surface, providing a novel strategy for SAG1 antigen detection and T. gondii diagnosis.

The materials and reagents used in this study and steps involved in SELEX techniques including the preparation of the SAG1 conjugate beads, the library and primers design, the selection steps, cloning and sequencing were given in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of dissociation constant of SAG1 aptamers
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the binding affinity of the selected aptamers for SAG1 antigen, various concentrations of fluorescein-labeled aptamers were prepared in binding buffer, heated to 90 °C for 10 min, cooled to 4 °C for 10 min, and then stabilized at 25 °C for 5 min. The processed aptamers were incubated with a fixed volume of SAG1-conjugated sepharose beads for 1 h. Following incubation, the beads were washed with binding buffer until no fluorescence signal was detected in the wash solutions. Target-bound aptamers were then eluted using an elution buffer, and the fluorescence intensity of the recovered DNA was measured. Binding curves were generated by plotting the input concentration of ssDNA against fluorescence intensity, and Kd values were determined using nonlinear regression analysis in Prism software.

The carbon screen-printed electrode (CSPE) used for the aptasensor fabrication consists of three components: carbon, which serves as both the working and counter electrodes, and silver, which functions as the reference electrode. The working electrode, with a diameter of 0.40 cm, was initially coated with GQDs (1 mg/mL, 5 μL) and then left to dry overnight at room temperature (RT). Aptamer immobilization onto the GQDs/SPE surface was achieved through physical absorption. In the immobilization time optimization experiments, 5 μL of 20 μM aptamer in binding buffer was incubated on the GQDs/SPE for durations ranging from 0 to 60 min at RT. During the concentration optimization experiments, aptamer concentrations ranging from 0 to 30 μM were incubated on the GQDs/SPE for 60 min. In the detection experiments, 5 μL of 20 μM aptamer underwent a 60-min incubation period on the GQDS/SPEs. Post-incubation, the electrodes were meticulously washed with a binding buffer then with 0.1 M Tris buffer to eliminate excess non-adsorbed aptamer molecules.

5 μL of the SAG1 protein solution, at the desired concentration ranging from 0.1 nM to 100 nM in the binding buffer, was incubated on the aptamer-modified GQDs/SPE at RT for 20 min. After incubation, the electrodes were washed with the binding buffer, and subsequent electrochemical measurements were performed. Cross-reactivity experiments were performed by incubating the aptamer-modified electrodes, prepared as described above, with a 1 nM concentration of various interferents individually such as influenza A virus (IFA V), influenza B virus (IFB V), and respiratory syncityal virus B (RSV B) in binding buffer. All the measurements were performed in triplicates and the relative standard deviations of the measurements were evaluated.

In the SAG1 biosensing experiments, the calibration curve was constructed based on the percentage increase in the oxidation peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox couple, which directly correlated with the concentration of SAG1 in the sample. For electrochemical measurements, the assays were conducted in a 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, redox solution using square wave voltammetry (SWV) at RT. SWV parameters were configured with an amplitude of 20 mV, an interval time of 0.05 s, a step potential of 5 mV, a scan rate of 50 mV/s, and a frequency of 20 Hz. In the optimization experiments, the histogram was plotted based on the percentage change in current (ΔI) in SWV determined using the formula: ΔI = (I0 − I) / I0 ∗ 100, where I0 represents the current observed without the aptamer and I represent the current measured with the aptamer. For the SAG1 protein biosensing experiments, the ΔI was calculated using the equation: ΔI = (I − Ia) /Ia ∗ 100, where Ia represents the background current of the aptasensor and I corresponds to the current measured after the SAG1 protein binding. All electrochemical studies were conducted in triplicate, and the results, including error bars, are shown on the detection and selectivity plots. For real sample analysis, a blood sample from a healthy volunteer (one of the authors) was collected and allowed to clot at RT for 30 min without shaking. Then, the sample was centrifuged at 1500 ×g for 15 min to separate the serum. The yellowish serum from the top layer was carefully pipetted into a sterile storage tube and utilized for subsequent studies or stored at −20 °C until further use. The collected serum was diluted at a ratio of 1:10 in PBS buffer and then spiked with different concentrations of the SAG1 antigen from 1 to 100 nM. The spiked serum samples were then incubated on the aptasensor and analyzed in triplicates using the above method.

The SELEX method was employed to identify new high affinity aptamers for the SAG1 antigen of T. gondii which can be utilized for the development of electrochemical biosensor. Scheme 1a provides a schematic representation of the SELEX cycle for identifying SAG1 aptamers and Scheme 1b illustrates the electrochemical aptasensing of SAG1 protein. The SELEX was performed according to the protocol reported previously [31,54–56]. A DNA aptamer library was designed, containing approximately 1015 unique sequences with 60 random nucleotides, flanked by primer binding sites. After synthesis, the DNA library was incubated with SAG1-positive Sepharose beads. Following the binding step, unbound sequences were washed away, and the selected ssDNA pool was eluted, desalted, and amplified via PCR. The forward primer included a fluorescein-labeled primer, enabling fluorescence measurements after each SELEX round to monitor aptamer enrichment. Meanwhile, the reverse primer featured a hexamethylene glycol linker extended with a poly-A20 chain, facilitating aptamer separation under denaturing conditions, as illustrated in Fig. S1.

As shown in Fig. 1a, the recovery of the DNA increased significantly up to round 6. After the sixth round, a counter-selection step was performed to remove DNA sequences binding to sepharose beads. In the counter-selection step, the DNA pool was incubated with the negative beads, followed by washing. The washes were then used in the subsequent regular SELEX cycle with the SAG1 beads. A significant decrease in DNA recovery was observed after the counter selection, indicating the successful removal of non-specifically bound aptamers. Subsequently, the recovery was slightly improved, reaching a plateau by round 11. At this point, the selection cycles were stopped, and the eluted DNA from the last round was cloned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cloning and sequencing of the aptamers
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the ssDNA aptamers from the last DNA pool, the DNA was cloned using the pUC8 IPTG LacZ B-galactosidase expression vector. After inserting the ssDNA aptamers into the vector and transforming them into competent E Coli cells, the selection of bacteria colonies containing vectors that successfully incorporated the ssDNA was performed by growing the colonies on agar media containing X-gal and IPTG. This selection system, known as the Blue-White System, enabled the identification of positive colonies. Bacteria containing the insert appeared as white colonies due to the induction of B-galactosidase expression by IPTG. This enzyme hydrolyzes X-gal into a blue product, allowing the differentiation of colonies with the insert. The clones were individually amplified and analyzed using a 2 % agarose gel, which revealed the migration of the aptamers at approximately 350 bp (Fig. S2). This confirmed the successful amplification of the clones and the presence of the desired PCR inserts.

A total of 18 ssDNA aptamer candidates (SOK1-SOK18) underwent sequencing, and their sequences were aligned using PRALINE software as shown in Fig. S3. Among these, six sequences (SOK13, SOK11, SOK16, SOK18, SOK14, and SOK10) were randomly selected and evaluated for their binding affinity. The secondary structures of the selected aptamers were analyzed using Mfold software. As illustrated in Fig. 1b–d, the secondary structures of aptamers SOK3, SOK14, and SOK18 displayed different stem-loops. Previous studies indicate that the primary binding sites in aptamers are typically stem-loops, hairpins, and other related structures. While the stems primarily serve to stabilize the aptamer's structure, the loops undergo conformational changes mediated by hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions when interacting with their targets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of aptamer binding affinity via fluorescence assays
ce:section.ce:para: The Kd values for six aptamer pools (SOK3, SOK11, SOK18, SOK16, SOK14, and SOK10) were determined by incubating varying concentrations (0–300 nM) of fluorescein-labeled individual aptamers with a fixed amount of SAG1 beads. The eluted DNA was quantified through fluorescence measurements. Saturation curves were generated and fitted using nonlinear regression analysis (1:1 binding model) in Prism software. All six aptamers demonstrated equilibrium Kd values in the nanomolar range: SOK3 (4.185 nM, R2 = 0.8153), SOK10 (152.9 nM, R2 = 0.7217), SOK11 (56.66 nM, R2 = 0.8128), SOK18 (4.034 nM, R2 = 0.8422), SOK16 (7.6 nM, R2 = 0.8704), and SOK14 (3.736 nM, R2 = 0.7367) as shown in Table 1 . Among these, SOK14 exhibited the lowest dissociation constant, as shown in the binding curve (Fig. 1e), indicating its superior affinity and specificity toward the target compared to the other aptamers.

To understand the difference in binding affinity between our aptamers and previously reported aptamers for the SAG1 protein, we compared our findings with those of Cui et al. [38], who developed several aptamers for this target. Among their designs, the optimized 53-nucleotide aptamer exhibited the lowest Kd value of 41.57 ± 9.74 nM, indicating a significantly higher binding affinity compared to their other aptamers. In contrast, our aptamer, SOK14, demonstrated a Kd value approximately ten times lower, highlighting its substantially stronger binding affinity for the SAG1 protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the fabricated aptasensors
ce:section.ce:para: Following the SELEX studies, a sensor substrate was fabricated using a GQDs modified screen-printed electrode (SPE) for the electrochemical aptasensing of the SAG1 protein. The identified aptamer SOK 14 from the SELEX process was physically adsorbed on the GQD/SPE to develop the aptasensor. After modifying the SPE with GQDs, physicochemical studies confirmed the structural features of the GQDs. Raman spectroscopy assessed the structural characteristics of the carbon lattice in the GQDs modified SPE, as shown in Fig. 2a. The D- and G-bands, indicative of A1g symmetry and E2g phonon in the bare SPE, were detected at 1325 and 1562 cm−1, respectively. In the GQDs/SPE, these bands showed slight shifts to 1351 and 1599 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, the I D/I G ratio measurements indicated variations in the sp2 in-plane graphitic carbon domains, recorded at 0.23 for the bare SPE and 0.78 for the GQDs/SPE, suggesting more defect sites after the modification of the SPE surface with GQDs [57]. SEM imaging was utilized to assess the morphology of both the bare SPE and the GQDs-modified SPE surface. The SEM images, presented in Fig. 2b and c, show a uniform flake-like structure on the bare carbon-based SPE surface (Fig. 2b) and a network structure formed by the GQDs on the SPE, with small GQD particles distributed across the electrode surface (Fig. 2c).

Fig. 3a shows the XPS deconvoluted spectra for the SPE, GQDs/SPE, and the aptamer-immobilized GQDs/SPE, illustrating the surface chemistry changes at each modification step. The spectra for the bare and GQDs-modified electrodes reveal the presence of carbon (C 1s) and oxygen (O 1s). Upon immobilization of the SOK14 aptamer, the appearance of a new nitrogen (N 1s) peak is observed, indicating successful attachment of the aptamer onto the electrode surface. The C 1s spectra of the bare SPE (Fig. 3b) exhibit distinct peaks at binding energies of 284 eV (C=C), 284.8 eV (CC), 286.0 eV (C–OH), and 288.3 eV (O–C=O), corresponding to sp 2 carbon, sp 3 carbon, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, respectively. Following GQD modification (Fig. 3c), a notable increase in the intensity of oxygenated functional groups is evident, suggesting the successful integration of GQDs onto the carbon SPE surface. This modification enhances the surface's oxygen content, potentially improving its biocompatibility. The C 1s spectra for the GQDs/SPE after aptamer immobilization (Fig. 3d) reveals the emergence of a new peak at 287.4 eV, attributed to the formation of a CN bond, confirming the presence of the aptamer. Additionally, the deconvoluted N 1 s spectra (Fig. 3e) provide further evidence of the presence of aptamers, showing the amine and imine bonds from the nitrogenous bases of the aptamer sequences. These results collectively confirm the success of the structural modification of the SPE with GQDs and the subsequent aptamer immobilization.

In preparation for the electrochemical aptasensing phase, the GQDs modified SPE was utilized to investigate the dependency of redox activity on the scan rate effect. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of GQDs/SPE were captured at varying scan rates ranging from 10 to 100 mV/s, as depicted in Fig. S4a. The corresponding derivative linear plot, correlating cathodic/anodic peak currents with the square root of scan rate (Fig. S4b), indicates that mass transport predominantly occurs through a diffusion-controlled process. Additionally, to ascertain the stability of the GQDs/SPE, CVs were continuously recorded during cycling 50 times, as shown in Fig. S4c. The stable redox behavior observed at the electrode-electrolyte interface suggests that the GQDs modified SPE exhibits enhanced stability on this electrode surface.

Scheme 1b illustrates the steps involved in the construction of SAG1 protein aptasensor. Upon confirming the stability of the GQDs modified SPE, it functioned as a sensor platform where the aptamers were physically absorbed through π − π stacking interactions between the GQDs and the developed aptamers. Three aptamers, denoted as SOK3, SOK14, and SOK18 based on their low Kd value, were specifically selected. Initially, CVs were recorded to observe the interaction of the aptamers with the GQD/SPE surface, followed by incubation with the SAG1 protein. As shown in Fig. 4a–c, the CVs of the GQDs modified SPEs indicate a decrease in current responses after interaction with the aptamers, due to the physical adsorption of the aptamer on the electrode surface. This reduction in peak current results from the electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphate backbone of the aptamers and the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−anions [50,52]. Following the addition of the SAG1 protein (0.1 nM), an increase in current response of Fe(CN)6]3−/4 is observed, indicating interaction between the aptamer and protein, as shown in Fig. 4c, where the SOK14 aptamer was utilized. There is a lesser increase in current response after interaction of the SAG1 protein and the SOK3 (Fig. 4a) and SOK14 (Fig. 4b) aptasensors. This increase in current is attributed to the release of the aptamer from the electrode surface upon target binding, driven by a conformational switch in the aptamer. This effect was notably pronounced in the SOK14 aptamer. These preliminary results suggest that SOK14 exhibits higher efficacy for electrochemical sensing of the SAG1 protein. To validate these results, SWV was conducted on the sensor platform constructed with SOK14 (Fig. 4d). The increase in current after the interaction between the aptamer and protein confirms that SOK14 is more effective compared to other aptamers. The current response after each step of the electrode modification and interaction between the aptamer and SAG1 protein, is presented in Table S1. The GQDs-modified electrode initially showed a current of 74.8 μA, which was decreased to 25 μA after aptamer incubation. Upon interaction with the analyte, the current increased to 32 μA, indicating the detachment of the aptamer from the electrode surface after analyte binding.

Prior to exploring concentration-dependent sensing, the constructed aptasensor was optimized in terms of incubation time and aptamer concentration. The immobilization step underwent meticulous optimization, monitoring the oxidation peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− following a 50 min incubation with varying concentrations of SOK14 aptamer (ranging from 0 to 30 μM) on the electrode surface. As shown in Fig. 5a, there was a noticeable decrease in the oxidation peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with increasing concentrations up to 30 μM. Specifically, the maximum reduction in oxidation peak current was observed at 20 μM after the 50 min incubation period, with subsequent increases in aptamer concentration yielding no further decreases (Fig. 5b). Consequently, an aptamer concentration of 20 μM was selected for all subsequent experiments to optimize the sensing of SAG1 via GQDs-modified SPE while minimizing potential nonspecific adsorption.

In a time-course experiment aimed at optimizing aptamer immobilization on the GQDs/SPE, the goal was to maximize changes in SWV oxidation peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− resulting from aptamer adsorption onto the electrode surface. The decrease in the oxidation peak of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− is illustrated in Fig. 5c. Following a 30 min incubation period with the aptamer, a reduction in oxidation peak by up to 23 μA (60 %) was observed, indicating strong affinity of the aptamer to the electrode surface, as shown in Fig. 5d. The most significant increase in SWV signal occurred after a 50 min incubation period, with no further decrease in current observed after 1 h of incubation. Therefore, 50 min was determined as the optimal incubation time for aptamer immobilization on the GQDs/SPE surface in the subsequent experiments.

After fine-tuned the conditions, the SWV response of the aptasensor was monitored across a spectrum of SAG1 concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 100 nM, focusing on the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− oxidation peak, as depicted in Fig. 6a. The SWV detected current changes and exhibited a linear response pattern. Fig. 6b illustrates a distinct linear correlation between the logarithm of SAG1 concentrations and the current changes (ΔI) observed in the SAG1 protein concentrations from 0.01 to 100 nM. This correlation is described by the equation ΔI = 48.32 + 16.75 × log [SAG1], with a correlation coefficient of 0.9963 (n = 3). The aptasensor's detection limit was conclusively determined as 11.5 pM in the binding buffer, defined by 3σ/slope (σ = standard deviation of blank samples). To evaluate the repeatability of the aptasensors, the Relative Standard Deviation (RSD %) of three measurements was calculated and found to be within the range of 3.3–4.9 %, indicating high reproducibility.

Previous studies by Cui et al. [38] and Aly et al. [58] utilizing ELAA, ELISA, nano-Gold ELISA, and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) have demonstrated only qualitative or semi-quantitative detection of SAG1 protein, as represented in Table 2 . Therefore, the LODs of these methods were not reported.

To determine the selectivity of the aptasensor platform, experiments were performed involving other biomarkers for infectious diseases, specifically, IFV A, IFV B and RSV B (10 nM). The specific protein interferents (IFA V, IFB V, and RSV B) were chosen for selectivity testing because they are commonly encountered in clinical samples and could potentially overlap with T. gondii infections. Including these interferents helps to assess the specificity of the aptasensor and ensures that it can distinguish between T. gondii and other pathogens that may present similar symptoms such as fever, fatigue, and respiratory distress. Their inclusion in the study enhances the relevance of the aptasensor for real-world applications, where co-infections with these pathogens are possible. SWV measurements assessed the aptasensor response to both SAG1 and the selected interferents. Fig. 6c illustrates the aptasensor response associated with SAG1 and the interferents, showing a significant increase in peak current with SAG1 compared to the nonspecific biomarker's samples. A t-test was performed to evaluate the statistical significance of the difference, revealing a p-value <0.05, confirming the significance of the observed differences. Spiked serum samples with SAG1 protein were tested to demonstrate the potential applicability of the developed aptasensor in field-deployable scenarios (Table S2). Favorable recovery percentages (90 to 95.1 %) were achieved for the spiked serum samples across varying concentrations (1 and 100 nM), indicating minimal interference from biological samples on the aptasensor.

In summary, this study developed a novel electrochemical aptasensor for the sensitive detection of SAG1, an essential biomarker for Toxoplasma gondii infections. Through SELEX technology, a new aptamer, SOK14, was identified for its high affinity and specificity toward SAG1 and successfully incorporated into a GQDs-modified SPE platform. Physicochemical characterization through Raman, SEM, and XPS studies confirmed the effective electrode modification, while electrochemical analyses substantiated the sensor's stability and sensitivity. The aptasensor demonstrated a linear response over a broad concentration range of SAG1, achieving a low detection limit of 11.5 pM. Selectivity tests conducted using interferents (IFA V, IFB V, and RSV B) and spiked serum samples showed minimal interference, highlighting the aptasensor's robust performance in complex biological matrices. This study represents a significant step toward developing a portable and cost-effective diagnostic tool for T. gondii infections, potentially enhancing early detection and clinical outcomes. Future directions for this research may include studies on aptamer truncations to identify the specific binding sites within the aptamer and remove nucleotides that do not contribute to binding. This is expected to enhance the binding affinity and sensitivity of the aptasensor. Additionally, further validation of the biosensor using patient samples, along with comparison to PCR results, will be crucial for improving its usability and performance in clinical settings.

Pandiyaraj Kanagavalli: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shahad Alkhaldi: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Mohammed Zourob: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shimaa Eissa: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S. Eissa acknowledges Khalifa University of Science and Technology, United Arab Emirates, for the financial support provided through start-up grant number 8474000456. M. Zourob acknowledges the support from Alfaisal University, Riyadh, KSA.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.143530.
